Year 2000 Billboard Chart Insights
An analysis of the 317 tracks entering the Billboard Hot 100 in the year 2000 reveals three standout records:

Fastest Climb to #1: Madonna’s “Music” reached the top spot in just 6 weeks (entering the chart at #41).
Slowest Climb to #1: Lonestar’s “Amazed” took 40 weeks of climbing to reach #1 (entering at #81).
Longest Run on Chart: Creed’s “Higher” set the endurance record, spending 57 weeks on the chart (peaking at #7).

title: NBA Scorigami
text:
A Scorigami is a final score combination that has occurred for the first time in league history. Out of 73,277 NBA games in this dataset, teams have registered 5,119 unique score combinations.

Home-Court Advantage: Home teams won 61.57% of all games, visible in the higher density of scores below the diagonal line (\(Home\ PTS > Visitor\ PTS\)).
Most Common Score: 105 - 99 (a home-team victory) is the most frequent score, occurring 100 times.
Extreme Outlier: The highest-scoring game occurred on Dec 13, 1983, when the Detroit Pistons defeated the Denver Nuggets 186 - 184 (370 total points) in triple overtime.

title: NBA Scorigami
text:
Scoring Trends and Scorigami Rates
How does the pace and scoring of the game affect how often we see a new final score? The charts below illustrate the relationship between the league’s average combined points per game and the rate of new Scorigami discoveries from 1946 to 2026.












Pace and Scorigami Insights

The Shot Clock Jump (1954): The introduction of the 24-second shot clock in 1954 caused average combined scores to surge from 159 to over 190 points in just a few seasons, causing a massive wave of new final scores (peaking at 178 new combinations in a single year).
The Scorigami Drought (1970s–2000s): As the league’s pace stabilized and the grid of common scores filled up, the discovery of new Scorigamis dropped below 30 per year in the early 2000s, coinciding with a low-scoring defensive era (averaging around 190 combined points).
The Modern Resurgence (2010s–2020s): High-volume three-point shooting and modern transition play have pushed the average combined points up to 225+. This modern offensive explosion has unlocked previously untouched high-scoring regions, causing new Scorigami rates to double to 40+ per year.

title: NBA Scorigami
text:
Deep Dive: NBA Scoring Analytics
To better understand the mechanics of NBA Scorigami, let’s explore three key visualizations detailing score frequencies, decade-by-decade probability patterns, and score margins.

1. The Most Frequent Scores
While Scorigamis represent the first time a score occurs, most NBA games cluster around a few very common score combinations.










2. Scorigami Probability by Decade
How hard is it to find a Scorigami? In the early days of the league, nearly every game resulted in a new score. By the 2010s, it became extremely rare. However, the 2020s are experiencing a sudden resurgence.










3. Distribution of Victory Margins
Due to home-court advantage, victory margins are heavily skewed in favor of the home team, with home wins outpacing visitor wins 61.57% to 38.43%.










Deep Dive Insights

The 6-Point Sweet Spot: The most common final score in NBA history is 105 - 99 (and its close neighbor 108 - 101), both representing a 6-point victory margin.
Decade U-Turn: The probability of a Scorigami dropped continuously for 70 years, hitting a low of 1.65% in the 2010s. However, the offensive explosion of the 2020s has reversed this trend, nearly doubling the probability to 3.12%.
The Home Shift: The distribution of victory margins is centered at a mean of +3.54 points in favor of the home team, indicating the strength of home-court advantage in final game outcomes.

title: NBA Scorigami
text:
Interactive Scorigami Explorer

Features

Filter by Team: Select a specific NBA franchise to view only their final score combinations.
Filter by Game Type: Isolate regular season matches, Play-in Tournaments, or NBA Finals.
Time-based Selection: Use the slider to filter scores within specific decades or seasons.
Hover Tooltips: Inspect individual scores to see occurrences, first/last dates achieved, and a sample game matchup.



How to Run Locally
To launch the dashboard locally in your R console by running:
shiny::runApp("app.R")

title: Million Song Dataset
text:
Echo Nest Music Metrics

Artist Familiarity: An algorithmic estimation (scaled 0 to 1) of how well-known an artist is to listeners.
Artist Hotttnesss: A measurement (scaled 0 to 1) of the artist’s current popularity or “buzz” at the time of data collection.

Missing Location Coordinates
A large portion of artists lack actual geographical coordinates, with their latitude and longitude set to a default placeholder of (0,0).



# A tibble: 2 × 2
  coordinate_type     n
  <chr>           <int>
1 placeholder      2493
2 usable           1418



Geographic Coverage Note
Because more than 60% of the artists in the dataset lack valid coordinates, the world map below only displays the subset of artists with usable location data.
